purpose: Realistic JSON fixtures representing structured output from each of ultrainit's 8 Phase 1 analysis agents (identity, commands, git-forensics, patterns, tooling, docs-scanner, security-scan, structure-scout). These fixtures stand in for real `claude -p` responses during testing, enabling phase-level and integration tests without making live API calls. They are wrapped in Claude response envelopes by `make_claude_envelope()` and served by the PATH-based mock `claude` binary.

architecture.overview: A flat directory of 8 JSON files — one per Phase 1 agent — plus a sibling `developer-answers.json` (one level up, in `tests/fixtures/`) representing Phase 2 interactive input. Each file is a minimal but realistic example of the structured_output field returned by its corresponding agent's `claude -p --output-format json --json-schema` call. Files are intentionally small (1–3 array items) to keep tests fast, not to simulate production-scale output. There is no subdirectory hierarchy; all finding fixtures live at the same level.

subdirectories.purpose: One JSON fixture per Phase 1 gather agent. Named to match the agent name used in run_agent() calls and the findings files written to .ultrainit/findings/ at runtime.
Parent directory also holds developer-answers.json (Phase 2 output) and a synthesis/ subdirectory with output-docs.json, output-tooling.json, and output.json for Phase 4 synthesis fixtures.

architecture.data_flow: Tests copy fixture files into $WORK_DIR/findings/ (mirroring the real .ultrainit/findings/ layout). The mock `claude` binary, when run in dispatch mode (MOCK_CLAUDE_DISPATCH_DIR set), reads the matching fixture by agent name, wraps it via make_claude_envelope(), and returns it as if it were a live Claude API response. Integration tests for synthesize_phase pass the findings directory fixtures as pre-populated inputs, then invoke the two-pass synthesis pipeline against them.

key_files.purpose: Minimal project identity fixture: name, description, languages (TypeScript/Python with percentages), frameworks (Next.js/FastAPI with versions), monorepo:false, deployment target. All required schema fields present. Used in nearly every integration and synthesize test.

Directory map fixture consumed by gather.sh to decide which modules get deep-dive agents. Contains 3 directories (src, src/components, tests) with role/priority/description. Has 5 schema compliance violations — the most broken fixture in the set.

Git history analysis fixture with hotspots, temporal coupling pairs, bug-fix density, ownership diffusion, recent activity, commit patterns (conventional commits + ticket references), and branch naming patterns.

Architectural patterns fixture listing detected patterns with type, evidence_files, description, and is_consistent flag. Contains a schema-invalid enum value (type: 'design' instead of 'architecture').
Code quality tools fixture (ESLint, Prettier) with config_path, enforced_in_ci, has_pre_commit. Missing the required 'purpose' field on each tool entry — a schema compliance gap.
Command catalogue fixture covering all 6 required categories (build, test, lint, format, typecheck, other). Each command has command, scope, ci_verified, source. Fully schema-compliant.
Protected files fixture with path_pattern, reason, safe_alternative for .env* and *.lock patterns. Fully schema-compliant and the most realistic fixture relative to its schema.
Documentation inventory fixture with a single README entry, documented_conventions array, and undocumented_gaps array. Fully schema-compliant but very sparse.
Sibling fixture (not in findings/) representing Phase 2 interactive developer input: project_purpose, deployment_target, never_do, common_mistakes, additional_context. Used in synthesize phase tests.

patterns.name: Minimal-but-typed arrays
patterns.description: Every array field contains 1–3 items regardless of what the agent prompt describes (e.g., 'top 20 hotspots' becomes 1 entry). This is intentional: tests exercise structure and I/O, not data volume. When adding new fixtures or extending existing ones, follow the same discipline — add only enough items to exercise the specific code path being tested, not enough to simulate a real project.

patterns.name: Response envelope wrapping
patterns.description: Fixtures represent only the structured_output field of a Claude API response, not the full envelope. Tests use make_claude_envelope() from test_helper.bash to wrap them: `{is_error: false, total_cost_usd: 0.15, structured_output: <fixture content>}`. Never put the envelope structure inside the fixture files themselves.

patterns.name: Filename = agent name = findings file
patterns.description: The fixture filename matches exactly the agent name passed as the first argument to run_agent(), which also becomes the findings file name under .ultrainit/findings/. This three-way identity (fixture → agent name → findings file) is load-bearing for dispatch mode in mock_claude.bash. Breaking any link breaks the mock dispatch routing.

patterns.name: Flat JSON objects at root
patterns.description: All fixtures are plain JSON objects (not arrays) at the root level. Fields are either scalar values, flat objects, or arrays of flat objects — nesting beyond 2 levels is rare. The only exception is commit_patterns inside git-forensics.json, which is a nested object. When adding fixtures, keep nesting shallow.

patterns.name: Schema-divergent fixtures used without validation
patterns.description: Five of eight fixtures have fields that violate their corresponding JSON schema (wrong enum values, missing required fields, extra fields). These pass tests because no runtime schema validation is performed on agent output — run_agent() just writes whatever jq extracts to the findings file. This means fixture violations are invisible at test time and only matter if schema validation is added later.

conventions.naming: Files are named in kebab-case matching the agent name: identity.json, git-forensics.json, docs-scanner.json, security-scan.json, structure-scout.json. Field names inside fixtures use snake_case throughout (ci_verified, change_count, co_change_count, path_pattern, safe_alternative, evidence_files, is_consistent). Boolean fields are plain booleans, not strings.
conventions.imports: Not applicable — these are static JSON files. Test files reference them via $BATS_TEST_DIRNAME/../fixtures/findings/<name>.json path patterns.
conventions.error_handling: No error handling inside the fixtures themselves. Test helpers handle the case where a fixture file is absent (treated as agent failure). The empty_json edge tests use deliberately malformed JSON (null root, empty object {}) to probe error handling in consuming code.
other: Numeric metric fields (change_count, fix_count, author_count, co_change_count) use integers, not floats.
All file path values inside fixtures use Unix-style forward slashes and are relative to project root (e.g., 'src/repos/user.ts', not '/absolute/path').
Boolean fields for CI and pre-commit state (ci_verified, enforced_in_ci, has_pre_commit) are always actual JSON booleans, never 'true'/'false' strings.
The developer-answers.json fixture sits one level above findings/ (in tests/fixtures/) to reflect its different origin phase (Phase 2 vs Phase 1).

internal: tests/helpers/test_helper.bash — make_claude_envelope() wraps fixture content into API response format
tests/helpers/mock_claude.bash — dispatch mode reads fixtures by agent name from MOCK_CLAUDE_DISPATCH_DIR
lib/agent.sh — run_agent() writes real findings to the same path layout these fixtures simulate
lib/gather.sh — Phase 1 orchestration references agent names that must match fixture filenames
schemas/ — Each fixture must conform to the schema of the same base name (identity.json → schemas/identity.json, etc.)

external_key.name: jq
external_key.purpose: Used in make_claude_envelope() and all test assertions to extract/transform fixture JSON. Fixtures must be valid jq-parseable JSON.

external_key.name: bats-core
external_key.purpose: Test framework that loads fixtures via file path; fixture filenames are referenced in @test bodies.

domain_terms.meaning: The JSON output file written by a completed agent to .ultrainit/findings/<name>.json. Fixtures simulate what these files contain.
The field inside a Claude API JSON response envelope that contains the agent's schema-validated answer. Fixtures represent this field's value only — not the full envelope.
Mock claude binary behavior where MOCK_CLAUDE_DISPATCH_DIR is set: the mock routes each call to a per-agent fixture file based on the agent name extracted from the call's arguments.
The outer JSON structure returned by `claude -p --output-format json`: {is_error, total_cost_usd, structured_output}. make_claude_envelope() adds this wrapper around raw fixture content.
A file with a high frequency of git commits — indicates churn or instability. Represented in git-forensics.json as {path, change_count}.
Two files that consistently change together in the same commits, suggesting hidden coupling. Represented as {file_a, file_b, co_change_count}.

The agent that maps the directory tree and assigns each directory a role (frontend-app, backend-services, etc.) and priority (high/medium/low). Its fixture drives which modules get deep-dive agents in Stage 2.

gotchas.issue: 5 of 8 fixtures violate their JSON schemas but tests still pass
gotchas.solution: No runtime schema validation exists in agent.sh — violations are invisible. Be aware that adding schema validation later will break these tests. When writing new fixtures, check the corresponding schemas/ file manually. Broken fixtures: patterns.json (type='design' invalid), tooling.json (missing 'purpose'), git-forensics.json (missing commit_patterns.example_messages), structure-scout.json (missing project_type/primary_language/architecture_summary, invalid role enum values, extra fields).

gotchas.issue: structure-scout.json uses human-readable role strings instead of schema enum values
gotchas.solution: The fixture has roles like 'source code', 'UI components', 'test suite'. The schema defines a strict enum: frontend-app, frontend-components, backend-app, backend-routers, backend-models, backend-services, tests, etc. Any code that pattern-matches on role values will silently fail to match. Fix by updating roles to valid enum values.

gotchas.issue: structure-scout.json has extra fields (total_files, total_directories) and the schema uses additionalProperties: false
gotchas.solution: If strict schema validation is ever enabled, these extra fields will cause validation failure. Remove them or update the schema to permit them.

gotchas.issue: Fixture filenames must exactly match agent names passed to run_agent()
gotchas.solution: The three-way identity (fixture filename = agent name = findings file) is load-bearing for mock dispatch routing. If an agent is renamed in gather.sh, the fixture file must be renamed too, or dispatch mode will serve the wrong fixture (or no fixture, causing a test failure that looks like an agent crash).

gotchas.issue: Fixtures contain only the structured_output value, not the full Claude API envelope
gotchas.solution: Always wrap fixtures with make_claude_envelope() before using them in mock dispatch files or as direct MOCK_CLAUDE_RESPONSE values. Passing raw fixture JSON where an envelope is expected causes jq extraction failures in run_agent().

gotchas.issue: git-forensics.json field naming: fixture uses 'path' but internal schema docs say 'file'
gotchas.solution: The actual JSON schema property is 'path', so the fixture is correct. The CLAUDE.md documentation describing the schema is slightly inconsistent. Trust the schema file, not the CLAUDE.md description.

gotchas.issue: developer-answers.json is NOT in findings/ — it's one level up in tests/fixtures/
gotchas.solution: It represents Phase 2 output, not Phase 1. Code that populates $WORK_DIR for synthesize tests must copy it to $WORK_DIR/developer-answers.json (not $WORK_DIR/findings/developer-answers.json). Placing it in the wrong location causes synthesize context-building functions to use empty/default values silently.

commands.purpose: Run unit tests that exercise fixture-consuming functions (synthesize_helpers, agent_run, merge) inside Docker
Run full bats suite including integration tests that load all 8 finding fixtures via mock dispatch
Inspect the most schema-divergent fixture to verify its current state
Quick check of top-level keys to verify required fields are present

skill_opportunities.name: fixture-sync
skill_opportunities.description: Validate all fixtures in tests/fixtures/findings/ against their corresponding schemas/ files, report mismatches (missing required fields, invalid enum values, extra fields), and optionally auto-fix them. This would catch the 5 current schema violations and prevent future drift.
workflow_steps: For each fixture file, find the matching schema in schemas/ by base name
Run jq to validate the fixture against the schema
Report fields present in schema but missing from fixture (required fields)
Report enum values in fixture that don't match schema enum lists
Report extra fields when schema has additionalProperties: false
Optionally generate a corrected fixture and diff it against the original
